# What the Architect Owns {#sec-what-architect-owns}

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Architecture 2.0 changes how architecture work is divided, but it does not make consequential decisions ownerless. AI systems, tools, specialists, and automated checks may formulate questions, create alternatives, run experiments, reject invalid candidates, and prepare recommendations. Named people or organizational roles must still own system intent and scope, abstraction choices, evidence standards, permitted automation, exceptions, final approval, ownership transfer, and later review. This is a present engineering rule for building answerable systems, not a claim that today's boundary between human and machine work is permanent.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What architecture work can be delegated, and which decisions and
responsibilities must remain assigned to named people or organizational roles?
:::

Architecture 2.0 permits a great deal of work to move, fundamentally shifting the boundary of human work, but the flow of accountability remains unchanged. The 'Lens of AI' flow in architecture follows a strict three-part logic. First, human architects own the decision; no model or tool can commit the organization to a result. Second, the bottleneck in automated workflows is transferring tacit knowledge and explicit authority across design phases. Third, AI can organize this knowledge, but it cannot own the risk. While an agentic system can coordinate several passes through the toolchain, only a named role can authorize a waiver or approve a signoff artifact.

The distinction is between delegated work and assigned responsibility. Work is delegated when another person, tool, or AI system is permitted to perform it, whereas responsibility is assigned when a named person or role must set the governing conditions, judge the evidence, authorize the action, and answer for what follows. One architect need not hold every right; large programs may divide workload scope, compiler compatibility, physical signoff, security, and product acceptance among several roles. These assignments still have to compose into a decision that someone can explain, challenge, transfer, and reopen. This chapter states that requirement as a current engineering rule, which does not depend on a permanent limitation of AI systems.

Automation may earn broader authority within a validated boundary, but organizational authority, accepted risk, and answerability must remain assigned while organizations, regulators, customers, and other engineers still depend on named roles to carry them. The following learning objectives outline the specific competencies required to navigate this shifting boundary, establishing what an architect must master to responsibly employ AI-driven workflows while maintaining system integrity.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can:

- **Distinguish** architecture work that may be delegated from responsibilities that must remain assigned;
- **Allocate** proposal, execution, rejection, waiver, recommendation, and final decision rights independently;
- **Transfer** ownership without losing unresolved claims, assumptions, exceptions, versions, or reopening conditions;
- **Make** tacit architecture knowledge reviewable without pretending that every expert judgment can be fully encoded;
- **Specify** the shared resources and team competence needed to sustain Architecture 2.0 work;
- **Defend** a bounded answer to the Lighthouse prompt and identify the questions the field has not yet settled.
:::

## Return to the Lighthouse

The book began with a deliberately ambitious prompt to illustrate the bounds of automation:

> Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable
> CPU, accelerator, or SoC block under a 3\ W TDP target in a 3\ nm-class LP
> mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected
> alternatives.

The prompt names a workload class, ISA family, power target, process, and
deliverable. It does not specify which tasks and deadlines establish workload
coverage, which software and ISA behavior must be preserved, which alternatives
deserve comparison, or which tool path could support the proposed claim. It
also leaves physical evidence, rejected candidates, exceptions, and decision
rights unspecified. Those omissions are not details that an architect adds
after generation. Resolving them is the architecture work.

The Lighthouse prompt serves as a prime example of why an architect's role evolves rather than disappears. The following callout explains how a simple prompt must be transformed into a rigorous study brief by resolving omitted details.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="The prompt has become a study brief"}
"XRBench-class" requires a versioned workload and a stated coverage claim.
"64-bit RISC-V-based" requires compatible compiler, simulator, and instruction
semantics. CPU, accelerator, and SoC alternatives imply different integration
contracts. The power and process targets require physical evidence appropriate
to the proposed action. The report must preserve serious comparisons and
rejected alternatives. Named roles must decide what is fixed, what automation
may change, which checks can stop the work, who may accept an exception, and who
may authorize the next step.
:::

As the next decision changes, so do the permitted automated actions, evidence
checks, rollback paths, and decision rights. They should change with feedback
latency, reversibility, and consequence. Assignment and answerability must
survive those changes rather than being inferred anew at every tool or
organizational boundary.

An AI system could help resolve each question. It might summarize workload
traces, propose a representation, generate RTL, call a simulator, organize
tool reports, or critique the evidence. The architecture study still needs a
role that owns the intended decision and people who understand the relevant
interfaces well enough to challenge the result. A prompt-to-chip demonstration
asks whether a system can produce an impressive artifact. An architecture
study asks whether another architect can determine what the artifact
establishes, why it should be believed, and what action it justifies.

Evidence fidelity and decision authority remain separate. A proxy, cycle-level
simulator, RTL test, emulator, synthesis flow, place-and-route result, or
signoff check may support a claim within its scope. Stronger evidence does not
grant permission to approve a waiver or authorize tapeout. Earlier chapters
related evidence burden to consequence in @tbl-evidence-by-consequence and
separated proposal, execution, rejection, waiver, recommendation, and approval
in @tbl-decision-rights-matrix. The finale joins those two ideas. The evidence
must fit the action, and the right to take that action must be assigned.

## Delegate Work and Assign Responsibility

AI systems, tools, and specialists may participate across all six architecture
capabilities. They can help formulate the problem, explore alternatives,
implement candidates, evaluate results, test explanations, and prepare a
defense. Validated checks may reject candidates or advance routine transitions
without waiting for a person to repeat the check. Delegation becomes unsafe
when the boundary of that authority, the evidence it relies on, or the route
for exceptions disappears.

Here, *the architect* means the authorized architecture role answerable for the
decision. It does not mean one individual performing every operation. The role
may be held by a person, a review body, or several named owners whose rights and
handoffs are explicit. No model, tool, specialist, or committee acquires those
rights merely by contributing work. The organization must assign them.

@fig-architect-owned-boundary separates work that may be delegated from the
responsibilities that must remain assigned. The upper band contains substantial
technical work. The middle row contains instruments that make the work easier
to inspect. The lower band remains visible across the entire study because no
artifact, card, check, or replay package owns the resulting decision.

![**Delegated work and assigned responsibility.** Within a defined study, AI systems, tools, and specialists may assist formulation, exploration, implementation, evaluation, explanation, and defense. Named roles remain responsible for scope, abstraction, evidence standards, automation authority, escalation, final approval, ownership transfer, and reopening.](images/F11-architect-owned-boundary){#fig-architect-owned-boundary width="85%" fig-alt="Architecture study containing the six architecture capabilities, delegated assistance, an architectural claim for review, supporting instruments, and a full-width band of assigned responsibilities."}

The upper band converges on an architectural claim with a proposition, scope,
evidence, counterevidence, and qualification. That claim, rather than the volume
of generated artifacts, is what another architect reviews. The middle row
organizes iteration, provenance, replay, and checks. The lower band assigns the
decisions that make those instruments meaningful. @tbl-architect-responsibilities
turns those assignments into records a reviewer can inspect.

| **Assigned responsibility** | **Why it remains assigned** | **Work that may be delegated** | **What another architect must be able to inspect** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **System intent and scope** | The study must serve a real decision rather than an available benchmark or proxy. | Elicit constraints, compare formulations, and find omissions. | Objective, non-goals, workload slice, hard constraints, and responsible role. |
| **Abstraction and representation** | Encoded state determines what a method can see, change, or simplify incorrectly. | Translate artifacts, organize traces, propose schemas, and identify gaps. | Exposed design space, hidden state, assumptions, and representation limits. |
| **Evidence standard** | Evidence must fit the claim, consequence, reversibility, and cost of being wrong. | Gather measurements, replay baselines, estimate uncertainty, and route anomalies. | Tool contracts, evidence scope, counterevidence, and escalation threshold. |
| **Automation and exceptions** | Someone must define what may stop, advance, reopen, or override a transition. | Apply validated checks, reject invalid candidates, and advance routine work. | Authority assignments, check coverage, exception rationale, expiration, and waiver holder. |
| **Final decision and lifecycle** | Product, tapeout, deployment, and cross-layer consequences require an answerable owner. | Summarize tradeoffs, monitor drift, and trigger revalidation. | Decision, rejected alternatives, residual risks, transfer record, and reopening condition. |

: **Delegation does not erase assignment.** Work can move among people, tools,
and AI systems while the governing responsibilities remain attached to named
roles. {#tbl-architect-responsibilities tbl-colwidths="[18,25,27,30]"}

The evidence standard should rise with the action under review. A simulator can
support a bounded design-space claim without supporting tapeout. Logical
verification can establish functional properties without establishing
routability, timing closure, power integrity, or thermal acceptability. The responsible role must understand enough of the delegated abstraction and tool
path to know which claim the evidence can support. It need not run every tool
or inspect every timing path personally.

To safely delegate work without losing control of the design, architects must perform regular boundary checks that focus on organizational constraints like signoff artifacts, waivers, and the Design-Loop Card as the boundary of human accountability. The checkpoint below emphasizes the need to define explicit limits and escalation paths for every automated action, using the L2 cache subsystem as a guiding example.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Check the delegation boundary"}
For every action available to an automated method, identify the constraints, checks, stop conditions, escalation route, and authorized owner. If the L2 cache size is tunable, an area-budget check may reject oversize configurations and a congestion screen may route risky candidates to physical review. Neither check may silently authorize a change outside its declared scope; the Design-Loop Card records these limits so that human accountability remains explicit.
:::

The requirement creates an institutional problem. Delegating routine work can
remove the practice through which experts once learned to detect unusual
failures. Bainbridge described this irony of automation long before current AI
systems [@Bainbridge1983Ironies]. A program that cannot identify someone who
understands what a check establishes, what it misses, and when it should be
challenged cannot responsibly grant that check broad authority. Training and
review capacity are part of the architecture system.

## Decision Rights and Ownership Transfer

Proposal, execution, rejection, waiver, recommendation, and final decision are
different rights. A model may propose an L2 cache organization. A harness may run
it. A timing or compatibility check may reject it. A specialist may recommend
the surviving L2 cache candidate. A waiver holder may accept a failed condition for a bounded reason. A product or tapeout role may authorize the next transition.
Holding one right does not imply any of the others.

::: {.callout-field-note title="Field Note: Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) Error Budgets"}
**Observation:** SRE separates the right to *propose/build* (Developers/AI) from the right to *authorize deployment* (SRE/Architects) using an objective contract: the Error Budget.
**Context:** AI can rapidly generate artifacts, but it cannot absorb organizational accountability. The bottleneck is safely transferring tacit context and explicitly approved exceptions (waivers) across organizational boundaries without rubber-stamping broken assumptions.
**Lesson:** If an AI agent burns through its "Waiver Budget," its authority to auto-advance is algorithmically revoked, forcing human escalation.
:::

This separation matters when work crosses hardware, software, package, and
product boundaries. A compiler lead may own whether the current lowering path
supports an instruction. A physical-design lead may own a congestion or power
integrity rule. A product architect may own a workload or battery obligation.
The architecture decision composes those judgments without pretending that a
single expert knows every layer.

Responsibility also has to survive a handoff. Sending a report, repository, or
signoff artifact is not enough. The new owner must receive the state of the
argument and the conditions under which it remains valid. The transfer items in
@tbl-ownership-transfer keep open work attached to a responsible role.

| **Transfer item** | **Question the handoff must answer** |
|---|---|
| **Claim and current decision** | What has been established, what action was authorized, and for what scope? |
| **Unresolved work** | Which claims, anomalies, alternatives, or integration questions remain open? |
| **Assumptions and validity limits** | Which workload, software, ISA, process, package, and deployment conditions does the decision depend on? |
| **Exceptions** | Which failed conditions or waivers were accepted, by whom, for what scope, and until when? |
| **Versions and provenance** | Which model, dataset, tool, environment, constraint, and artifact versions produced the evidence? |
| **Monitoring and reopening** | Which observation, drift, failure, or version change requires another review? |
| **New assignment** | Which person or organizational role now owns the decision, exceptions, monitoring, and next review? |

: **Ownership transfer carries the argument.** A handoff is complete when the
new role can locate the open obligations and knows what would invalidate or
reopen the decision. {#tbl-ownership-transfer tbl-colwidths="[27,73]"}

Leveson's systems view treats safety as a property of interactions in a
sociotechnical control structure rather than as the sum of reliable components
[@Leveson2012EngineeringSaferWorld]. Most architecture studies are not safety
cases, so the transfer is limited. The useful lesson is that a capable component
or passing check cannot establish the behavior of the surrounding decision
system. Scope, evidence, exceptions, handoffs, and authority must work together.

Historical failures highlight the danger of ambiguous ownership during critical design handoffs, demonstrating why transferring unresolved exceptions and assumptions is just as vital as transferring the artifacts themselves.

::: {.callout-field-note title="Field Note: The Pentium Chronicles and the Transfer of Risk"}
**Observation:** In Bob Colwell's account of the Pentium FDIV bug (*The Pentium Chronicles*), a small omission in early design validation became extraordinarily expensive to reverse after shipment.

**Context:** The technical failure was compounded by ambiguous ownership during critical design handoffs. As designs move from architectural specification to pre-silicon validation and ultimately to manufacturing, the unresolved exceptions and assumptions must transfer alongside the RTL.

**Lesson:** At each consequential transition, a human had to own the evidence standard and the authority to proceed. AI cannot absorb organizational accountability; it can generate the tests and summarize the waivers, but it cannot own the risk of proceeding.
:::

::: {.callout-field-note title="Field Note: The Normalization of Deviance"}
**Observation:** If exceptions (like simulator waivers or timing violations) aren't rigorously tracked and formally handed off between teams, anomalous behavior silently becomes the new standard.
**Context:** AI can rapidly generate artifacts, but it cannot absorb organizational accountability. The bottleneck is safely transferring tacit context and explicitly approved exceptions (waivers) across organizational boundaries without rubber-stamping broken assumptions.
**Lesson:** This proves why an architect must actively own the lifecycle of every exception an AI encounters, rather than letting the AI auto-resolve it.
:::

## Make Tacit Knowledge Reviewable

Architecture constraints do not all begin in a specification. Engineers also
rely on tacit knowledge, which Polanyi distinguished from knowledge that can be
fully stated [@Polanyi1966TacitDimension]. A packaging engineer may know that a
distributed L2 cache partition threatens the power-delivery mesh. A compiler lead may know
that the current LLVM backend cannot efficiently schedule prefetching for a non-blocking L2 cache. A senior
architect may anticipate that an L2 cache's coherency traffic will starve
the XR display controller. A local proxy may miss all three failures.

The goal is not to claim that every judgment can be converted into a field in a
schema. It is to expose enough of a consequential judgment for another person
to understand where it came from, where it applies, who validated it, and when
it needs another review. AI can search design reviews, waiver histories, and
tool reports or help an expert articulate missing cases. A named domain owner
must decide which proposed constraint enters the architecture state.

If the represented state omits a package limit, unsupported lowering path,
coherency constraint, or display deadline, the method may optimize an invalid
candidate. Once validated, some knowledge can become an environment constraint
or rejection check. Other judgments should remain qualified expert assessments
rather than false precision.

@fig-tacit-extraction-bridge shows the boundary. AI-assisted elicitation can
organize situated knowledge and flag conflicts. It does not write directly into
authoritative state. A domain expert approves, rejects, or qualifies the entry.
Conflict, expiration, a tool upgrade, a new workload, or new physical evidence
sends it back for review.

![**Reviewing tacit knowledge before using it.** AI can help elicit and organize domain knowledge, but a named domain expert must validate constraints, assumptions, and rejection conditions. Conflicts, expiration, or new evidence require another review.](images/F10-tacit-extraction-bridge){#fig-tacit-extraction-bridge width="85%" fig-alt="Diagram showing domain knowledge passing through AI-assisted elicitation and domain validation before entering represented study state, with conflict, expiry, and new evidence returning the record for revalidation."}

Each retained judgment needs a source, scope, owner, and revalidation
condition. A constraint tied to one LLVM revision, process design kit, workload
mix, or approved waiver cannot silently become a permanent rule. The architect
answerable for the larger decision need not perform every specialist task, but
must know which judgments can change the result, who validated them, and how
they will be revisited.

## Objections

The first objection is that this framework overformalizes ordinary architecture
work. Provenance, baselines, rejection criteria, held-out validation, and
structured assurance all predate Architecture 2.0
[@ChenPenneyJimenez2020AIArchitecture; @Kelly2004GSN;
@ISO2018Iso26262; @PeherstorferWillcoxGunzburger2018Multifidelity]. The objection
is right about that lineage. The added burden appears when AI helps formulate
the question, create alternatives, select tool actions, interpret results, or
write the claim. The record should be proportional to consequence. A small,
human-run comparison may need only a concise run record. A delegated workflow
that can advance candidates faster than reviewers can reconstruct the evidence
needs stronger structure.

A more fundamental objection is that AI may add no architectural value to a
particular study. In the completed array study in @sec-executed-scale-study,
the AI and conventional arms tied, and the proposed mechanism did not survive
the declared contrast. A no-change result, a simpler baseline, or a decision to
stop is legitimate when the evidence supports it.

The second objection is that assignment will slow capable experts and useful
automation. It can. The answer is not to require manual approval for every
transition. Validated checks can reject candidates, routine work can advance,
and specialized reviews can be delegated. Review should concentrate on changes
of scope, weak evidence, exceptions, cross-layer conflicts, difficult-to-reverse
actions, and ownership transfer. The cost of review should itself be measured.

The third objection is that naming a human owner creates false confidence.
Correct. A signature does not make weak evidence strong, and authority without
a reason is not architecture. The named role must be able to state what was
decided, which evidence and counterevidence mattered, what remains unresolved,
and what would reopen the decision. Assignment creates a challenge path. It
does not guarantee that the decision is correct.

The fourth objection is that reserving final authority for people reflects only
the limits of current AI systems. That may eventually be true for some technical
transitions. The rule in this chapter is narrower. An organization must assign
authority and answerability under its current technical, legal, and product
conditions. If future systems earn broader authority, the evidence for that
authority, its operating boundary, its failure handling, and the role that
authorized it will still require explicit treatment.

Not every architecture problem needs AI, a design loop, or a card. When AI does
help produce or evaluate an architectural claim, another architect must still
be able to inspect the claim, its evidence boundary, and the authority behind
the resulting action.

## Shared Infrastructure

Architecture 2.0 cannot rest on individual prompts and isolated success
stories. It needs shared resources that let teams learn across studies while
preserving the boundary between public, protected, and unreleasable evidence.
The specific resources will change. Their architectural function is more
stable. In fields that struggle with irreproducibility, data leakage, and
unvalidated proxies, establishing shared, versioned infrastructure can provide a
foundation to ensure claims can be independently verified.
@tbl-field-maintenance groups them by the information they must
preserve.

| **Shared resource** | **What it must preserve** | **Why it matters** |
|---|---|---|
| **Tasks, datasets, and trajectories** | Versioned workloads, contexts, actions, observations, successful and failed runs, rejected alternatives, and coverage limits. | A model needs more than final designs. It needs the decisions, feedback, and failures that produced them. |
| **Representations and embeddings** | Source artifacts, schema or encoder version, provenance, missing fields, update policy, and tests for whether architectural distinctions survive encoding. | A vector or graph is useful only if teams can determine what information it preserved, collapsed, or allowed the method to change. |
| **Environments and tool interfaces** | Runnable or inspectable contracts, versions, constraints, observations, warnings, costs, failure modes, and fidelity transitions. | Data detached from the tool path cannot show what an observation means. |
| **Matched baselines and evaluation cases** | Conventional methods, comparable budgets, held-out tasks, cross-tool checks, and separate measures of architecture outcome and AI contribution. | Improvement cannot be attributed to AI when the comparison changed the tools, feedback, or opportunity to iterate. |
| **Challenge and protected-review paths** | Independent reviewers, disclosure boundaries, conflict rules, appeals, retest paths, and a public account of what protected review established. | Proprietary evidence may support an internal decision without supporting public replay or a public ranking. |
| **Lifecycle records** | Maintainers, release snapshots, drift checks, negative results, revalidation rules, and retirement policies. | Workloads, tools, models, and rejected alternatives age. Stale resources should not silently govern new decisions. |

: **Infrastructure for cumulative architecture work.** The field needs linked
resources that connect a question and representation to tools, feedback,
evidence, challenge, and lifecycle ownership. {#tbl-field-maintenance tbl-colwidths="[21,44,35]"}

Public comparison, protected inspection, and replay establish different
things. Shared tasks and baselines support comparison. A qualified reviewer may
inspect restricted evidence. Replay additionally requires runnable inputs and
permission to execute them. None can stand in for another.

These resources come from several places. Public benchmarks and open-source
tools provide common tasks and interfaces. Research artifacts provide
experimental trajectories, baselines, and negative results. Industrial design
reviews, regressions, waivers, and post-silicon telemetry contain valuable
failure knowledge, though much of it requires protected review or a redacted
public account. Synthetic tasks can isolate mechanisms or stand in for
proprietary constraints, provided the record does not claim equivalence to the
private design.

Representations deserve special care. An embedding derived from RTL, traces,
compiler state, floorplan features, or review text is not a timeless fact about
the architecture. It is an output of a particular encoder, source set, version,
and objective. Teams should retain the link back to those sources and test
whether distinctions that matter to the decision remain recoverable. If a
coherency assumption or package constraint disappears in the encoding, more
training data will not repair the missing state by itself.

An undifferentiated archive of model output and tool logs is not a dataset for
architecture reasoning. A reusable record connects the architecture question,
represented state, candidate actions, tool observations, failed alternatives,
interpretation, and decision. Logs contribute measurements and provenance. The
architectural claim and its limits still have to be supplied.

Maintaining this infrastructure is an organizational responsibility. Someone
must decide when a benchmark version changes, when protected evidence is
sufficient for a local decision, when a failed alternative should be retested,
and when a resource should be retired. Governance and incentives for that work
remain open research and institutional questions.

## Building Team Competence

A team does not need to build the field's entire infrastructure before it can
begin. It needs one bounded architecture question and one study whose evidence
another architect can challenge. The initial record should identify:

1. the decision and a meaningful baseline;
2. the represented state, allowed actions, hidden assumptions, and non-goals;
3. a versioned environment and at least one credible tool path;
4. the role assigned to AI and the conventional alternative;
5. the feedback and review budget;
6. a bounded architectural claim and the evidence that would contradict it;
7. the people or roles allowed to reject, waive, recommend, decide, and reopen.

The first study may end with a no-change result, a failed tool path, or a decision not
to use AI. Those outcomes still build competence if the team can explain what
was tried, what was learned, and which evidence changed the decision. A design
loop card can index that work, but the architecture question, tool path,
evidence, explanation, and ownership are the substance.

Competence should be tested on an unfamiliar but tractable architecture
problem, not by recall of the book's vocabulary. Constructive alignment asks
assessment to measure the work learners are expected to perform
[@Biggs1996ConstructiveAlignment]. Here that means bounding a question, choosing
and connecting methods and tools, preserving serious alternatives, evaluating
with matched evidence, testing a mechanism account, and defending a limited
recommendation. Classic quantitative architecture remains the grounding
discipline [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. The progression in
@tbl-team-competence makes that performance observable without turning it into
a vocabulary test.

A graduate should be able to complete that study outside the examples used in
the book. The test is whether the graduate can defend the architecture question,
method choice, tool evidence, mechanism account, rejected alternatives, and
bounded recommendation under informed challenge. A justified finding that AI
added no value is a valid result.

| **Stage** | **Observable performance** |
|---|---|
| **Begin** | Complete one bounded comparison with a baseline, versioned tool path, failed-run record, evidence limit, and assigned decision rights. |
| **Develop** | Handle an unfamiliar problem, challenge the representation and proxy, compare AI and conventional methods fairly, and explain a no-change or negative result. |
| **Lead** | Allocate rights across specialists and automated systems, judge exceptions, transfer ownership, and maintain the study as workloads, tools, and responsible roles change. |

: **Competence appears in the work.** A learner or team demonstrates
Architecture 2.0 by completing and defending an unfamiliar architecture study,
not by reproducing its terminology. {#tbl-team-competence tbl-colwidths="[20,80]"}

The irony of automation returns here. Teams need enough direct contact with
tools, failures, and cross-layer effects to supervise the work they delegate.
Education cannot stop at prompting, and expert practice cannot stop at final
approval. Both require repeated exposure to anomalous evidence, invalid
comparisons, failed abstractions, and decisions that must be reopened.

# Open Problems in Architecture 2.0

The proposed assignments answer a current engineering need. They do not settle
the technical or institutional design of Architecture 2.0. Several questions
now become first-class research problems.

1. **World models and representations.** Which representations let an AI system predict the consequences of architecture actions across workload, software, microarchitecture, physical design, and deployment? How should teams test what the representation omitted?
2. **Multi-fidelity feedback.** When should a method trust, calibrate, challenge, or abandon a proxy as evidence arrives from simulation, synthesis, physical implementation, and silicon?
3. **AI contribution.** Under matched tools, human time, compute, and feedback budgets, how can studies separate better artifacts from better architectural insight and better decisions?
4. **Review capacity.** Which allocations of automated checks, specialists, independent reviewers, waiver holders, and decision owners catch consequential errors without making review the limiting resource?
5. **Changing targets.** How should evidence age when compilers, generated software, fleet workloads, process assumptions, or product requirements change after a design decision?
6. **Protected evidence.** Which claims can be supported through authorized inspection when proprietary designs, training context, or tool results cannot be released, and which public claims remain unjustified?
7. **Lifecycle authority.** How should organizations transfer, revalidate, or retire decisions when models, environments, representations, and responsible roles change?

Self-improving systems sharpen every question. A future system may revise its
own architecture representation, repair a tool interface, choose a new proxy,
or reorganize failure records. The same changes can hide failures, overfit an
evaluation, or weaken a check. Each material change therefore needs a
validation path and an assigned role that determines whether earlier evidence
still applies.

Earlier Architecture 2.0 work described a horizon of increasing autonomy
balanced with human expertise [@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20]. The hard
problem is not choosing one side of that balance forever. It is learning how
technical authority can expand without making claims, evidence, exceptions,
and responsibility harder to inspect.

## Returning to the Architecture Question

The Lighthouse prompt can support a real architecture study. It cannot, by
itself, specify one. Someone must choose the workload coverage, software and ISA
contracts, alternatives, representation, tool path, evidence threshold, and
decision the study is meant to inform. AI can help with every one of those
choices. It can also generate artifacts, run experiments, find failures, and
prepare the argument.

What the architect owns is not every keystroke or tool invocation. The
architect owns the coherence of the decision. Named roles must ensure that the
problem being solved is the intended one, the representation exposes the
relevant state, the tools return evidence appropriate to the claim, serious
alternatives and failures remain visible, exceptions are explicit, and the
authorized action follows from the record. When ownership changes, those roles must transfer the open obligations and the conditions that could reverse the decision. To verify that ownership remains intact after AI integration, teams must evaluate their assignment structures using concrete tests to ensure that accountability and challenge pathways survive the transition to automated workflows.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Test the assignment"}
- **Assignment test.** Can a reviewer identify who set scope, authorized automated actions, judged evidence sufficiency, approved any exception, and made the final decision?
- **Transfer test.** Can the next owner find unresolved claims, assumptions, rejected alternatives, waivers, relevant versions, monitoring conditions, and reopening triggers?
- **Challenge test.** Can a qualified person dispute the evidence or interpretation and reach the role authorized to respond?
:::

That answer is intentionally bounded. It does not prove that people will always
retain the same technical tasks or approval rights. It says that work should
not become ownerless while its consequences remain organizational and
physical. Delegation can expand as methods, checks, and evidence improve. The
assignments should change deliberately, with their justification and operating
limits available for review.

Architecture 2.0 is the engineering discipline of using AI, grounded in
architectural representations, tools, and experiments, to formulate, explore,
implement, evaluate, explain, and defend computer architecture decisions. However, the ultimate purpose is not merely to automate design, but to elevate it. AI may produce much of the evidence and argument, accelerating our ability to explore vast design spaces. The field succeeds when another architect can understand what was claimed, determine what the evidence supports, challenge the decision, and know who is responsible for what happens next. The architect's true ownership lies in ensuring that every system is built with unyielding integrity, clear answerability, and a steadfast commitment to delivering systems that serve their intended purpose.
